segments.s131-g89: به جای is an idiom meaning "instead of" (preposition + noun), so the phrase begins with the prepositional phrase. بزرگی و تخت are the nouns ("greatness and the throne") and نشست is the past tense of نشستن meaning "sat" or "came to rest"; in Persian the verb comes at the end (SOV). Here نشست indicates that what follows (پشیمانی و درد) has taken the place of greatness and the throne.
segments.s131-g90: پشیمانی و درد is a nominal phrase "remorse and pain." بودش is the past tenseِ بودن with the enclitic -ش (= "to him/for him"), so literally "it was to him"; together with the following prepositional phrase به دست it yields the idiom "was in his hand/was his lot." Note the enclitic marks possession or reference to the person implied by the context.
segments.s131-g91: بمانده is the past participle/predicative form of ماندن ("remained/stayed") used here as a verbal predicate meaning "he remained." The location follows: به بیشه درون = "in/at the inner grove" (به + place + درون "inside"). زار و خوار are descriptive adjectives "woeful/wretched and humiliated/despised" that qualify his state.
segments.s131-g92: نیایش is the noun "prayer". همی is the classical/historical imperfective/habitual particle (still/continually) that, when combined with a past-tense verb, gives a repeated or ongoing action. کرد is the past of کردن (here "did/prayed"), and با کردگار = "to/with the Creator/God." Together: "he kept praying to the Creator."
segments.s131-g25: یکی کار: a nominal phrase meaning 'one thing' or 'a certain affair' (ici poetic). ماندست is an archaic/poetic fused form meaning 'remains' (مانده است); کاندر جهان is a locative/phrase 'in the world' (کاندر = ک + اندر = 'in/within'), so the segment literally says 'one affair remains in the world.' Word order is subject + verb + locative, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s131-g26: نشان تو هرگز نگردد نهان: نشان 'mark/sign' is the object, تو is the possessor/target ('your'), هرگز 'never' negates, نگردد is the present/subjunctive/passive sense of گشت 'become' (here 'become/turn into'), and نهان 'hidden'. Together: 'your sign will never become hidden.' The verb نگردد is in a classical present/jussive form used to express general/future negation.
segments.s131-g27: چه دارد همی آفتاب از تو راز: چه دارد = 'what does ... have' (interrogative + present verb دارد). همی is a poetic adverb meaning 'still' or ongoing aspect. آفتاب is the subject 'the sun', and از تو راز literally 'a secret of/about you' (از here marks the matter/subject). Word order places the verb before the rest for rhetorical emphasis.
segments.s131-g28: که چون گردد اندر نشیب و فراز: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/which / when'), چون here functions as 'as/when', گردد is the present/subjunctive of گشت 'turns/comes to be', and اندر نشیب و فراز is a locative phrase 'in/through descent and ascent' (نشیب = downhill/decline, فراز = rise/height). The clause describes the sun's movement through its low and high arcs.
segments.s131-g29: چگونست ماه و شب و روز چیست: چگونست is an archaic fused form of 'چگونه است' meaning 'how is/what is the condition of'. The list 'ماه و شب و روز' are the items under question (moon, night, day). چی ... ست (چیست) is an emphatic copula/question 'what is it', a rhetorical doubling common in classical Persian to intensify the inquiry about their state.
segments.s131-g30: برین گردش چرخ سالار کیست: برین = بر + این ('upon this' / 'in this'), گردش 'turning/circuit', چرخ سالار = 'the heavenly/wheel-lord' (چرخ here = wheel/sky; سالار = lord/master). کیست is 'who is (it)'. The phrase asks who governs or is master of this rotation of the heavens.
segments.s131-g31: دل شاه ازان دیو بی‌راه شد: 'دل شاه' = 'the king's heart'. ازان (از + آن) = 'because of that' / 'from that (demon)'. دیو is the agent/causal noun 'demon', and بی‌راه شد literally 'became off the path' = 'was led astray' or 'went wrong'. شد is simple past/perfect 'became'. The construction expresses causation: because of that demon the king's heart went astray.
segments.s131-g32: روانش ز اندیشه کوتاه شد: روانش = 'his soul/mind' with enclitic -ش 'his'. ز = 'from/because of', here marking the source or separation. اندیشه 'thought', کوتاه شد literally 'became short' — an idiom meaning his mind was cut off/short of thought, i.e. he lost his train of thought or was deprived of reflection. The verb شد is past 'became'.
segments.s131-g33: گمانش چنان شد که = literally 'his guman (thought/guess) became such that...'. گمانش is the noun گمان with the possessive clitic -ش ('his'), چنان شد is a past-tense copular phrase ('became so') with شد as the past form of شدن. که introduces the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.s131-g34: به گیتی = 'to/for the world' (prepositional phrase). مراو را is marked by the object/topic marker را; نمودست is an archaic/poetic past-form meaning 'showed/appeared' (نمود + است fused), and چهر ('face, appearance') is the noun denoting what was shown — word order is inverted/poetic, with the verb نمودست appearing before the noun phrase چهر.
segments.s131-g35: ندانست is the negative past of دانستن ('he did not know'). کاین (= که + این) introduces the content clause 'that this...'. چرخ را uses را as a definite/topic marker attached to چرخ ('the heaven/sky'), and مایه نیست is the predicate 'has no source/means' with نیست ('is not') negating existence/possession.
segments.s131-g36: ستاره فراوان = 'the stars are many' (noun + adjective). The coordinating و links that clause to ایزد یکیست, where ایزد ('God/deity') is the subject and یکیست is a verblike predication 'is one' (یکی + است fused) — a compressed, poetic statement 'stars many, God one.'
segments.s131-g37: همه is the universal subject 'all/everyone'. زیر فرمانش is a prepositional/relational phrase 'under his command' (فرمان + possessive -ش). بیچاره‌اند is the present plural predicate 'they are miserable/helpless', with the enclitic plural/present ending -اند.
segments.s131-g38: که introduces an explanatory clause 'for/that'. با governs the list of afflictions ('with'): سوزش ('burning' — here suffering/burning), و جنگ ('and war'), و پتیاره‌اند — the final predicate 'they are p.t.i.a.r.e' (پتیاره‌اند) is a poetic plural predication meaning 'they are wretches/wretched', so the clause explains why they are miserable.
segments.s131-g39: جهان‌آفرین is a compound noun 'world-creator' (the Creator). بی‌نیازست is a fused predicate 'is free from need' (بی‌نیاز + است) meaning self-sufficient. ازین is a poetic/demonstrative 'from these/of these' (از + این written as ازین), marking what He is free from.
segments.s131-g40: ز بهر تو = a poetic sequence meaning 'for your sake' (ز = poetic 'from/for', بهر = 'for/sake', تو = 'you'). باید is the modal 'must/it is necessary', and سپهر و زمین are the coordinated nouns 'heaven and earth' governed by that necessity — literal sense: 'for your sake heaven and earth must (be/act).'
segments.s131-g41: پراندیشه (an adjective/compound) precedes the copula شد forming a predicate meaning 'became anxious/overwrought'. جان آن پادشا is the grammatical subject phrase ('the life/soul of that king' = idiomatically 'the king'), with آن as a demonstrative/definite modifier and پادشا an archaic form of 'king'. The past/correlative شد marks a change of state ('became').
segments.s131-g42: که introduces a subordinate clause that gives the content of the anxiety ('that / how'). تا چون شود is an old-fashioned clause order where تا + چون + شود = 'how it will be / how it comes to be' with شود a present/subjunctive/predicative verb 'become/be'. بی پر is a prepositional phrase 'without feather(s)/wings' and اندر هوا uses the archaic preposition اندر + noun to mean 'in the air'.
segments.s131-g43: ز دانندگان is a prepositional phrase 'from the knowledgeable/experts' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). بس بپرسید here is an instructional/polite phrase 'ask fully/ask enough' where بپرسید takes the imperative/polite form and شاه appended as a vocative or topic ('O king' / 'the king (asked)'). Word order (verb before vocative/topic) is typical of classical narration.
segments.s131-g44: کزین خاک is a compound prepositional/locative 'from this earth' (کزین = 'from this'). چندست is the old interrogative 'how many/what is the measure' used to ask distance; تا is a marker 'to/until' here indicating extent/distance, and چرخ ماه 'the wheel/sky of the moon' means the moon's celestial sphere or orbit. The sequence expresses the question 'how far is it from this earth to the moon's sky?'.
segments.s131-g45: ستاره شمر is an epithet 'the astrologer (literally 'star') Shamr' (noun + proper name). گفت and شنید are past-tense narrative verbs 'spoke/said' and 'heard/listened' respectively; the coordinating و connects the two actions ('...said and Khosrow heard'). This is straightforward past narration in classical Persian.
segments.s131-g46: یکی is an indefinite subject 'someone/a certain one'. The two adjectives کژ و ناخوب precede the noun چاره and together describe that noun ('a crooked and bad remedy'); گزید is the simple past 'chose' and applies to the subject. Classical order places qualifiers before the noun and the verb after.
segments.s131-g47: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said' used to report an order. پس is a simple connective 'then/afterwards'. تا به هنگام خواب forms a temporal phrase 'at/unto the time of sleep' with تا indicating the temporal point or limit and به هنگام خواب = 'at the time of sleep/bedtime'.
segments.s131-g48: برفتند is the past plural 'they went/departed'. سوی is the preposition 'toward'. نشیم عقاب is a classical/genitive-style phrase 'the eagle's perch/resting-place' (نشیم here functions as a place noun in classical usage) and follows the preposition; عقاب specifies whose perch it is. The clause as a whole reports their movement toward that place.
segments.s131-g49: This is a past‑tense main clause: برداشتند is third‑person plural past ('they took/removed'). ازان ('of those / from them') is a demonstrative pronoun referring back to previously mentioned young, and بچه here means 'young (animals) / offspring'. بسیار modifies بچه and here means 'many'. Word order is standard Persian: prepositional/determiner phrase (ازان بچه بسیار) precedes the past verb برداشتند.
segments.s131-g50: به هر خانه‌ای is a dative/locative phrase 'to/into each house/household' (به + هر + خانه‌ای). بر دو بگذاشتند is the verb phrase 'they placed/put (بگذاشتند) two (بر دو)' — here بر two functions as the numeral 'two' (with بر marking quantity) and the verb is past plural. The whole segment answers where/how many they put.
segments.s131-g51: همی is a poetic/progressive particle meaning 'continually / kept on', and it scopes over the verb پرورانیدشان ('they reared/raised them') in past tense. سال و ماه is a parallel time expression 'years and months' indicating duration; the whole clause means 'they kept raising them for a long time.'
segments.s131-g52: This is an adverbial phrase describing how they were fed: به مرغ و به گوشت بره literally 'on fowl and on lamb meat'. The repeated به marks the two kinds of food. چندگاه at the end is an adverb meaning 'for a while / for some time', limiting the temporal scope of this diet.
segments.s131-g53: چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause; نیرو گرفتند is a past compound meaning 'they gained strength' (گرفتند = 'took/gained'). هر یک 'each one' is an apposition, and چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion' (second چو = 'as/like'). The clause reads 'When each had become strong, each like a lion...'.
segments.s131-g54: بدان سان که is a fixed concessive/causal phrase meaning 'in such a way that / so that'; here ب is the prefix be‑, دان is the root in the idiom, and سان means 'manner/like'. غرم is used poetically as 'wrath/fury' and آوریدند is the past plural 'they brought/caused'. زیر at the end functions adverbially as 'under / subject to' (i.e., put under control/under them). The clause therefore describes the consequence of their strength: they caused wrath/force to be put under (their sway).
segments.s131-g55: ز is the poetic form of از 'from' introducing material. عود here is used as 'oud/wood' (a material) and قماری modifies it (literal sense from the lexicon is 'gambling' but here it functions as an adjectival/identifying epithet tied to the wood name). یکی تخت کرد is straightforward past tense 'he made one throne' (یکی = one, تخت = throne/bed, کرد = made). The segment describes what he constructed and from what material.
segments.s131-g56: سر درزها را is the definite object ('the head/top of the seams' with را marking the object). به زر سخت کرد is a causative/result phrase: به زر 'with/by gold' and سخت here functions adverbially 'firmly / strongly' (or 'with hard gold'), followed by کرد 'he made/did' — so 'he fastened/secured the seam‑heads with solid gold.' The verb کرد repeats as the finishing action of the line.
segments.s131-g65: چو (= when) introduces a temporal clause; شد is the past of شدن 'become', so 'چو شد ... عقاب' = 'when the eagle became ...'. تیز پران is a compound adjective placed before the noun (literally 'sharp-winged' → 'swift-winged'), modifying عقاب (eagle).
segments.s131-g66: سوی + noun = 'toward X'; کردند is past plural 'they did/made' used here with شتاب (haste) as its object ('made haste'). هر یک modifies the (implied) subjects: 'each one'. The word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect: 'Toward the meat they made, each, haste' → 'each hurried toward the meat.'
segments.s131-g67: ز + روی زمین = 'from the surface of the earth' (ز in a poetic register = 'from'). تخت is the direct object 'throne' and برداشتند is past plural 'they lifted/removed'. Word order is prepositional phrase + object + verb.
segments.s131-g68: ز هامون = 'from the plain' (هامون = plain). به ابر اندر افراشتند = 'they raised (it) into/to the cloud(s)'; here به + اندر together give a movement/toward sense ('into/onto the clouds'), and افراشتند is past plural 'they raised/exalted'.
segments.s131-g69: بدان حد که = 'to that/so great an extent that' (بدان is a fused form meaning 'to that'); شان here functions as a possessive/demonstrative ('their' or 'their dignity/status'), بود is past 'was', and نیرو به جای means 'strength/power was in place' or 'their strength was set in its place.' The clause gives a measure or limit ('to such a degree that ...').
segments.s131-g70: سوی گوشت = 'toward the meat'; کردند is past plural 'they did/made'. آهنگ here means 'intention/aim' and رای means 'counsel/plan'—the phrase کنند آهنگ و رای = 'they made/set their intention and counsel' i.e. 'they turned toward the meat with resolve and plan.'
segments.s131-g71: شنیدم is a first-person singular past verb ('I heard') and is followed by the conjunction که introducing a reported clause. In the subordinate clause, کاووس is the subject and شد is the past of شدن used here intransitively/predicatively (poetically 'ascended' or 'was set/placed'); بر فلک is a directional/locative prepositional phrase ('to/upon the sky/heaven') following the verb. The phrase بر فلک شد is an idiomatic, elevated/poetic way to say he rose to the heavens.
segments.s131-g72: همی رفت combines the particle همی (a classical/poetic continuous marker meaning 'kept on/continued') with the past رفت to express an ongoing past action ('he continued/kept going'). تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'until' and is followed by رسد, here a present/subjunctive 3rd-person form of رسیدن used in classical Persian after تا to mean 'he should/does reach' (i.e. 'until he reached'). The doubled بر (before رسد and before ملک) is a poetic/directional use: بر رسد بر ملک = 'reach/attain to the realm/kingdom', with ملک (realm) as the goal following the verb.
segments.s131-g81: نگونسار (an adjective) + گشتند (past plural of گشتن) form a predicative clause: ‘they became mournful.’ ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’ introducing the source, and ابر سیاه is the noun + adjective ‘black cloud(s).’ The subject (they) is not explicitly stated here but is understood as the clouds; adjective-before-verb order and ز + noun phrase are typical of Ferdowsi’s elevated/poetic word order.
segments.s131-g82: کشان is a present participle meaning ‘dragging’ and expresses manner; it attaches back to the (implied) subject from the previous clause. بر + زمین here gives direction/placement ‘onto / down to the ground,’ از هوا is a source phrase ‘from the air/sky,’ and تخت شاه is a genitive construction ‘the king’s throne.’ Word order is compressed: participle + direction + source + object.
segments.s131-g83: سوی is the preposition ‘toward,’ followed by بیشه (‘grove’). شیرچین is a noun modifying بیشه (a compound/epithet: ‘milkmaid’s grove’ or ‘the milkmaid Grove’). آمدند is the past plural verb ‘they came’ (subject again implied from context). The sequence is straightforward direction + place + motion verb.
segments.s131-g84: به آمل gives the destination ‘to/at Amol.’ بروی here works as a compound/verb of motion meaning to go onto/onto the surface (classical usage: ‘to alight/upon’) and is followed by زمین ‘ground,’ while آمدند is the main past verb ‘they came.’ Together the phrase means ‘they came down onto the ground at Amol.’
segments.s131-g85: نکردش is the negative past verb نَکرد + enclitic object ش (him/it): ‘did not (do) him’ = ‘did not harm/undo him.’ تباه is a predicate adjective/noun ‘ruined/undone.’ از شگفتی جهان is an ablative/cause phrase ‘by/from the marvels/wonders of the world.’ The clause thus says that the world’s wonders did not destroy him.
segments.s131-g86: همی is an archaic adverb meaning ‘still/always’ placed before the substantive; بودنی is a deverbal noun ‘being/existence’ (literally ‘that-which-is’) serving as the object of داشت (‘had’). اندر نهان is a locative phrase ‘within/hidden.’ The sequence literally reads ‘always a being he had within hidden,’ i.e. he possessed a hidden, enduring life/being.
segments.s131-g87: سیاووش is the subject (proper name). زو is the prepositional phrase ‘from/of him’ (زو = ز + او). خواست is the past tense ‘he wished/sought/asked.’ کاید پدید is a noun (کاید ‘plot’/‘device’) followed by پدید (‘to appear/manifest’), forming the object clause ‘that a plot be made manifest.’ The verb خواست takes that-clause object in this classical idiom.
segments.s131-g88: ببایست is an archaic modal/impersonal past form meaning ‘it was necessary’ / ‘he had to.’ لختی is an adverb ‘for a short while.’ چمید and چرید are two coordinated past-tense verbs joined by و; they describe successive actions (‘...chamid and chared’), with چمید here functioning as a past verb (the exact lexical nuance is rare/poetic) and چرید meaning ‘grazed.’ Grammatically, the modal governs the coordinated verb phrase that follows.
segments.s131-g9: بگرداندش is a past, causative-style verb form with the enclitic object -ش ('him'), literally 'made/caused him to turn.' The noun phrase سر ز یزدان پاک follows as the thing turned: سر 'head' governed by the preposition ز 'from' (poetic 'from / away from'), and یزدان پاک 'the Pure God' is the source; word order here places the verb before its object phrase, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s131-g10: فشاند is a simple past verb 'he scattered/cast.' It takes a prepositional phrase بر آن فر زیباش ('upon that fair far') introduced by بر 'on/upon'; فر 'farr' (royal glory) is modified by زیباش 'beautiful/fair' which follows the noun in this poetic order. خاک 'dust' is the thing scattered and appears after the verb phrase; overall this reads 'he scattered dust upon that fair farr.'
segments.s131-g11: شنیدند is a masculine plural past verb 'they heard.' The conjunction و links the clauses. بر دل گرفتند یاد is an idiomatic construction: گرفتند 'they took/seized' with the locative phrase بر دل 'upon the heart,' and یاد 'memory/remembrance' is the thing taken — literally 'they took remembrance upon their hearts' = 'remembrance seized them.' Note the object یاد appears after the verb phrase, a typical flexible word order in poetry.
segments.s131-g12: کس here functions as 'no one' (negative indefinite). از بیم is a prepositional phrase 'out of fear / because of fear' where از takes بیم 'fear' as its object; کاووس is the name in the genitive relation to بیم ('fear of Kavus'). پاسخ is the direct object 'answer/reply,' and نداد is the past negative verb 'did not give' (literally 'gave not'); together: 'No one, for fear of Kavus, gave an answer.'
segments.s131-g13: یکی is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an individual). دیو is the noun 'demon,' modified by the epithet دژخیم (an intensifying adjective, 'cruel/torturous'). بر پای خاست is a verb phrase: خاست 'rose/stood up' with the locative phrase بر پای 'to (his) feet' (literal 'on foot'), giving 'a certain cruel demon rose to his feet.'
segments.s131-g14: چنین at the clause head is an adverb 'thus/so.' The expected verb گفت 'he said' appears next (here listed with senses that do not include 'said' — see senses mapping). کاین is the fused particle که + این meaning roughly 'that this / that' introducing the quoted content. چربدستی is a noun 'sleight-of-hand / dexterity,' and مراست is effectively 'is mine' (a contracted copular phrase: مرا + است); word order is standard for reported speech: 'Thus he said: "This sleight-of-hand is mine."'
segments.s131-g15: غلامی is a noun meaning a young man/servant (here 'a youth/servant drawn from his own retinue'). بیاراست is the verb 'he adorned/arrayed' (past), bi‑arast; از خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'from his own / from himself' where خویشتن refers to his own people/retinue. The verb precedes the source phrase in this poetic ordering.
segments.s131-g16: This is a short nominal description: سخن‌گوی is 'a speaker' (one who speaks/eloquent), و 'and' links the two adjectives/roles, شایسته 'worthy' modifies انجمن 'assembly/meeting.' Together they qualify the غلامی as 'a speaker and fit for the assembly.' Note the adjective شایسته follows and agrees in sense, as is common in Persian descriptive phrases.
segments.s131-g73: This clause gives the main action: دگر ('again/then') is an adverbial temporal marker placed before گفت (here intended as ‘said’) to signal a new action or utterance. ازان رفت بر آسمان is a compact past-tense clause: ازان = 'from there' (place source), رفت = past 3rd-person singular 'he went', and the preposition بر + آسمان ('to/upon the sky') shows direction/goal. The word order (source — verb — goal) is normal in poetic Persian.
segments.s131-g74: که تا introduces purpose: که + تا here function together as a purposive clause 'so that / in order that'. جنگ سازد is a present/subjunctive-like clause where سازد (3rd-person singular of ساختن) means 'make' or 'wage' and جنگ is its object ('war, battle'). به تیر و کمان uses به in the instrumental sense 'by/with' followed by تیر 'arrow' و 'and' کمان 'bow' to mark the weapons used.
segments.s131-g75: ز هر گونه‌ای هست آواز این is an existential/attributive statement: ز 'from/of' + هر گونه‌ای 'every kind/variety' modifies آواز 'voice/sound', and هست is the copula 'is/exists'. The demonstrative این ('this') follows the noun to pick out the previously mentioned voice. Word order (modifier — copula — noun — demonstrative) is typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s131-g76: نداند is the negative form meaning 'knows not' (3rd-person), followed by the exclusion phrase به جز 'except'. پر خرد is a compound where پر 'feather' is qualified by خرد 'wisdom' (i.e., 'the feather of wisdom' or 'the wise-feathered one'). راز این 'this secret' is the object: راز = 'secret' and این = 'this' (demonstrative). The clause says that nobody knows the secret except that wise feather/figure.
segments.s131-g77: پریدند بسیار is a past plural verb phrase: پریدند 'they flew' and بسیار modifies it as 'in great numbers' or 'much/many'. و joins the two coordinated verbs; ماندند 'they remained' is the second past plural verb. باز here follows ماندند and functions adverbially ('back/behind' or roughly 'remained behind' / 'still remained'), a common poetic placement that intensifies or localizes the idea of remaining.
segments.s131-g78: چنین باشد is a formulaic jussive/optative sense 'so shall it be' or 'thus it is' (چنین = 'thus/so', باشد expressing a subjunctive/jussive flavor). آنکس که ... is a relative clause: آنکس 'that person' + که 'who' + گیردش 'takes it' (گیرد = he takes/captures; -ش = it). آز is the object/adjunct meaning 'greed', so the clause predicts the fate of the person who seizes it out of greed.
segments.s131-g79: چو is a temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. با مرغ پرنده is a prepositional phrase 'with the flying bird' where مرغ 'bird' is further specified by the adjective/epithet پرنده 'flying creature'. نیرو نماند is the predicate: نیرو 'strength' + نماند (negative/remaining verb) meaning 'no strength remained' or 'had no strength left.' The whole clause is a temporal clause setting the condition for the main clause that follows.
segments.s131-g80: This is the result clause: غمی گشت 'grief became' (گشت = became/turned) followed by پرهاب خوی درنشاند. پرهاب is an adjective/noun meaning 'fearsome' or 'dread', خوی 'disposition/nature', and درنشاند is a causative/past form meaning 'placed into' or 'settled in'. Together the phrase says that grief turned into dread and settled into its nature/disposition (i.e., it instilled fear in its nature).
segments.s131-g1: چنان بد که is a result clause opener: چنان (so) + بد (bad/evil) + که (that) = 'so... that'. ابلیس is the subject. روزی پگاه is a temporal adverbial ('one day/at dawn') following the subject; روزی here conveys a single occasion (archaic/poetic) and پگاه specifically pins it to dawn.
segments.s131-g2: این جمله نهاد + مفعول + فعل دارد: 'یکی انجمن کرد' = 'he held an assembly' where یکی is indefinite 'a certain/one', انجمن is the object and کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/held'. پنهان ز شاه is an adverbial phrase 'hidden/from the king' using poetic ز (= از) to mark separation/secrecy.
segments.s131-g3: به دیوان چنین گفت is 'he said thus to the divan/assembly' (به + indirect object, سپس گفت past-tense verb). کامروز کار is topical and begins the next clause ('today the affair...'), placed after the verb as the topic of the following predication.
segments.s131-g4: به رنج و به سختیست is the predicate: two prepositional phrases (به رنج 'with/to suffering' and به سختی 'with difficulty') followed by سختیست (سختی + است contracted), meaning 'it is hard'. با شهریار ('with the king') specifies the party involved; word order is topical/predicative rather than strict modern prose.
segments.s131-g5: یکی دیو is the indefinite subject 'a certain demon'. باید is the modal 'must/ought', کنون is the adverb 'now', and نغزدست is a fused adjectival/nominal predicate ('deft‑handed/skillful'); the structure is subject + modal + temporal + predicative adjective.
segments.s131-g6: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause modifying the demon or his quality: داند is present 'he knows'. ز here is the poetic preposition (= از) meaning 'of/about', followed by هرگونه رای و نشست 'every kind of counsel and meeting' as the object of knowledge.
segments.s131-g7: شود here functions as an auxiliary/modal giving potential or future force and is followed by the main action expressed by کند ('make/do'). جان کاووس is the direct object 'Kavus's life', and بیره ('a lamb') is a predicative noun: 'will make Kavus's life (into) a lamb', i.e. a victim/prey — a poetic metaphor.
segments.s131-g8: به دیوان is an indirect object 'for/to the divs' (diwan/divs — the exact sense 'demons' isn't in the provided sense list). بر + ین = برین 'on/upon this' (archaic spelling of بر این) attaches to رنج 'this hardship'. کوته کند is 'make short/cut short' (کوته + کند), the verb phrase meaning 'will shorten'.
segments.s131-g17: همی بود is a narrative past formula: همی is an adverbial particle (here intensifying/introducing the narrative) and بود is the past tense 'was'. تا یک زمان is a temporal phrase 'at one time / once', with تا marking the temporal boundary. شهریار (king) is the topic/subject placed after the time phrase in typical Persian word order.
segments.s131-g18: ز پهلو برون شد is a prepositional + verb phrase: ز پهلو 'from (his) side/seat' (ز = from, پهلو = side), برون 'out/forth' adverbially modifies the motion, and شد is the past verb 'went out/rose'. ز بهر شکار is a purposive phrase 'for the sake of hunting' (ز بهر = for/to, شکار = hunt). The order places the source and direction before the verb, as typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s131-g19: بیامد is the past intransitive 'came' (subject is generic/indefinite here). بر او is a prepositional phrase 'toward him' (بر + pronoun او). زمین بوس داد is a light noun + verb construction: زمین‌بوس (earth-kiss) with داد 'gave' used to mean 'gave a kiss' or idiomatically 'kissed the ground'—object (بوس / kiss) appears before the verb, which is common in Persian.
segments.s131-g20: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' (introducing an unspecified person). دسته گل is a compound noun 'a bouquet' (دسته = bundle/cluster, گل = flower). به marks the indirect object 'to/for Kavus' and داد is the past 'gave'. Word order is Subject (یکی) + object (دسته گل) + indirect object phrase (به کاووس) + verb (داد) as a straightforward transitive clause.
segments.s131-g21: چنین گفت is 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said' — note the lemma senses list provided seems to record 'گفت' as a noun but here it functions as the verb 'he said'). کاین is the fused archaic/conjunctive form of که + این meaning 'that this' or simply 'that', introducing a nominal clause. فر زیبای تو = 'your beautiful/beautiful farr' (فر = divine/royal glory, زیبای = beautiful, تو = your), which is the object/topic of the following clause.
segments.s131-g22: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed' / intensifier). چرخ‌گردان 'the turning/revolving sky' (literally 'wheel-turner') is the grammatical subject, سزد is an impersonal/presentational verb meaning 'it befits / it is fitting', and جای تو 'your place/station' is the object in the sense 'suits your station'. The order places the subject before the verb and the possessed noun phrase جای تو after the verb, typical of Persian poetic syntax.
segments.s131-g23: به کام تو is a dative/benefactive phrase 'to your wish / to your desire' (به = to/for, کام = desire, تو = your). شد is the past 'became/was made'. روی گیتی همه is a topicalized noun phrase 'the whole face/surface of the world' with همه ('all') postposed for emphasis — poetic inversion is common: literally 'the face of the world became all to your desire' meaning 'the whole world was turned to your will.'
segments.s131-g24: شبانی و گردنکشان is a coordinated noun phrase 'shepherds and the proud' (شبانی = shepherds, گردنکشان here = 'the proud/haughty ones'). چون introduces a simile 'like', and رمه 'a flock' completes it: the two classes of people are compared to a flock (i.e. behaving uniformly). The phrase is a brief simile attached to the preceding clause.
segments.s131-g57: This is a prepositional/locative phrase. به (preposition) introduces the goal/place; پهلوش is پهلو + possessive enclitic -ش (“his side”); بر (here = on/upon) begins the following locative complement; نیزهای دراز is a poetic spelling for 'نیزه‌ی دراز' (a long spear). Word order places the preposition and possessive before the locative noun phrase.
segments.s131-g58: ببست is a past-tense transitive verb 'he tied/fastened.' و simply coordinates clauses. بران‌گونه is an adverbial compound 'in that way / thus' modifying the following phrase. بر کرد ساز is literal sequence: بر (on/upon) + کرد (he made/did) + ساز (here 'a contrivance/device' rather than a musical instrument) — i.e. 'he made/rigged a device on it.'
segments.s131-g59: بیاویخت is a past verb 'he hung' (classical/poetic form). از here marks the relation 'from / on' with the following noun, so 'hung from the spear.' ران بره is a noun phrase 'the haunch/thigh of the lamb' (ران = haunch/thigh; بره = lamb). The verb precedes its complements, a common order in poetry.
segments.s131-g60: ببست (past 'he tied/fastened') is repeated and takes a following locative/intent phrase. اندر اندیشه = 'in (his) thought / in mind' where اندر = in/within and اندیشه = thought/intention; دل یکسره ('heart completely') is an ablative/ adverbial phrase meaning 'with his heart wholly/resolutely' — together: he tied it while his heart was wholly intent/decided.
segments.s131-g61: ازن پس is a fixed temporal phrase 'after that / thereafter' (ازن = from that; پس = after/then). عقاب is the noun 'eagle' followed by the postposed adjective دلاور ('valiant'), a common Persian poetic order where the adjective follows the noun. چهار is a numeral 'four' serving as the direct object (literal here; the precise referent is context-dependent).
segments.s131-g62: بیاورد is past 'he brought' (third-person). و connects the next action. بر تخت (on the throne) is the locative complement; بست is the past transitive 'he bound/tied' (takes the previously introduced object — 'them'); استوار is an adjective 'firm/secure' modifying the result (i.e. he bound them firmly/securely).
segments.s131-g63: نشست is the past of 'to sit'/'to take a seat.' از بر تخت is a classical/poetic locative phrase meaning 'on/upon the throne' (از بر here is formulaic in older poetry). کاووس شاه is a name + title construction ('Kavus, the king') with the title following the name.
segments.s131-g64: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'for/which,' linking to the previous clause. اهریمنش = اهریمن + possessive -ش 'his demon' (i.e. the demon belonging to or afflicting him). برده is the past participle/verb 'taken / carried off' (here 'had carried/led away'); بد دل = 'bad-hearted / ill-hearted' (an adjectival phrase describing his state); ز راه = 'from the path / off the way' (ز = from, راه = path). Together: 'for a demon had carried him off, his heart made evil and led him from the path.'

sentences.s131-s45: Instead of greatness and a throne there sat remorse; regret and pain were in his hand (were his lot).
sentences.s131-s46: He remained within the grove, wretched and humiliated, and continually prayed to the Creator.
sentences.s131-s13: One thing remains in the world whose sign of you will never be hidden.
sentences.s131-s14: What secret does the sun still have about you, that when it turns in descent and ascent?
sentences.s131-s15: What is the condition of the moon, night and day? What are they like; who is the lord of this turning of the heavenly wheel?
sentences.s131-s16: The king's heart was led astray by that demon; his spirit was cut off from thought.
sentences.s131-s17: His thought became such that the revolving heavens showed his face to the world.
sentences.s131-s18: He did not know that this heaven has no sustaining source; the stars are many, and God is one.
sentences.s131-s19: All are under his command and miserable, for they endure burning, war, and wretchedness.
sentences.s131-s20: The Creator of the world is free from need with respect to these things; for your sake heaven and earth must (be).
sentences.s131-s21: The soul of that king became anxious, wondering how it would be to be without wings in the air.
sentences.s131-s22: The king asked the learned men: from this earth, how far is it to the moon's sky?
sentences.s131-s23: Shamr the astrologer spoke and Khosrow heard; someone chose a crooked and bad remedy.
sentences.s131-s24: Then he commanded that, at bedtime, they go toward the eagle's perch.
sentences.s131-s25: They took many of those young and set two in every house.
sentences.s131-s26: They raised them for years and months, feeding them on fowl and on lamb's meat for a while.
sentences.s131-s27: When they had gained strength — each one like a lion — in such a manner that they brought wrath beneath (them).
sentences.s131-s28: From qamari wood he made a single throne; he fastened the tops of the seams with hard gold.
sentences.s131-s33: When the swift-winged eagle became hungry, each hurried toward the meat.
sentences.s131-s34: They lifted the throne from the surface of the earth; from the plain they raised it into the clouds.
sentences.s131-s35: To such an extent that their strength was in its place, each turned toward the meat with intent and counsel.
sentences.s131-s36: I heard that Kavus ascended to the heavens and kept going until he reached the kingdom.
sentences.s131-s41: The black clouds grew mournful and, dragging the king's throne from the air, brought it down to the earth.
sentences.s131-s42: They came toward the milkmaid’s grove; at Amol they descended to the ground.
sentences.s131-s43: The marvels of the world did not undo him; he had an enduring being hidden within.
sentences.s131-s44: Siyavash asked of him that a plot be brought forth; it was necessary that, for a short while, he nibble and graze.
sentences.s131-s5: He turned his head away from the Pure God and cast dust upon that fair farr (royal radiance).
sentences.s131-s6: They heard, and remembrance took hold upon their hearts; no one, for fear of Kavus, gave any answer.
sentences.s131-s7: A certain cruel demon rose to his feet and said: 'This sleight-of-hand is mine.'
sentences.s131-s8: He arrayed a youth from among his own — a speaker, and fit for the assembly.
sentences.s131-s37: Then he said, from there he went up to the sky so that he might wage war with bow and arrow.
sentences.s131-s38: This voice exists in every guise; none knows this secret except the feather of wisdom.
sentences.s131-s39: They flew in great numbers and many remained behind; such will be the fate of the one who takes it out of greed.
sentences.s131-s40: When the flying bird has no strength left, grief became dread and put (it) into its nature.
sentences.s131-s1: Things were so bad that Iblis, at one dawn, secretly held an assembly away from the king.
sentences.s131-s2: He said to the assembly thus: 'Today the business with the king is full of toil and hardship.'
sentences.s131-s3: There must now be a certain demon, deft‑handed, who knows every kind of counsel and assembly.
sentences.s131-s4: He will make Kavus's life a lamb (i.e., a victim) and thereby will cut short this hardship for the divs.
sentences.s131-s9: Once upon a time the king rose from his seat to go hunting.
sentences.s131-s10: Someone came before him, kissed the ground, and gave a bouquet to Kavus.
sentences.s131-s11: Thus he said: 'This beautiful farr of yours—indeed the turning heavens deem it fitting for your station.'
sentences.s131-s12: All the face of the world was set to your desire; shepherds and the proud alike were like a flock.
sentences.s131-s29: He fastened it to his side on a long spear, and in that manner made a contrivance upon it.
sentences.s131-s30: He hung the lamb's haunch from the spear and bound it, his heart wholly intent.
sentences.s131-s31: After that the valiant eagle brought four and securely fastened them to the throne.
sentences.s131-s32: He sat upon the throne of King Kavus, for a demon had taken him, his heart turned evil and led him from the path.
